title: Bramborové knedlíky
subtitle: Czech potato dumplings
excerpt: Mashed potato bound with flour and egg, shaped into smooth balls and boiled until light and pillowy. The second great Czech dumpling — denser than the bread version, with a clean potato flavour that holds up beside svíčková or any creamy sauce.
sourceNotes: Based on the Czech bramborový knedlík tradition as documented in Jiri Kopac and Pavel Maurer, 'Czech Cuisine' (1999). Potato dumplings are the standard accompaniment to svíčková na smetaně (beef in cream sauce) and appear widely across German, Austrian, and Central European cooking under various names.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked dumplings on a tray, then bag. Reheat by simmering in water for 5 minutes.

content.text: Bramborové knedlíky are made from a simple dough of cooked, riced potato, plain flour, and egg. The potato should be dry: boiled in its skin, then peeled and riced or mashed while still hot. Wet potato makes a dough that absorbs too much flour and produces heavy dumplings. The finished dough is shaped into smooth balls, dropped into boiling salted water, and cooked until they float and are firm throughout.

The quantity of flour varies depending on the water content of the potato. Start with less and add more only if the dough won't hold its shape. The dough should feel smooth and slightly tacky, not sticky — it should not cling to your hands.

items.prepNote: floury variety, boiled in skin then peeled and mashed or riced while hot
optional: added to the dough for a firmer texture

content.text: Boil {{potato}} of potatoes in their skins in well-salted water until completely tender. Drain, peel immediately, and pass through a potato ricer or mash thoroughly while still hot. Spread on a board or tray and leave for 5 minutes to steam dry. The potato must be as dry as possible before the flour goes in.

Transfer the cooled mashed potato to a large bowl. Add {{eggs}} beaten egg, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{semolina}} of semolina if using. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour a little at a time, mixing until a smooth, cohesive dough forms. It should hold its shape when rolled into a ball without sticking to your hands. If it is still sticky, add flour a tablespoon at a time.

Flour your hands lightly. Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces and roll each into a smooth ball. Bring a large pan of well-salted water to the boil. Lower the dumplings in gently. They will sink, then rise to the surface after 5–7 minutes. Once they are all floating, cook for a further 5 minutes. Lift out with a slotted spoon and rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Dumplings fall apart in the water
items.cause: Dough too wet, or too little flour
items.fix: The dough must be firm enough to hold a ball shape without any cracking at the surface. Add flour until the dough is smooth and dry enough to roll cleanly.

items.symptom: Dumplings are dense and gluey inside
items.cause: Too much flour, or potato still wet when flour was added
items.fix: Use the minimum flour needed. Ensure the potato is steamed dry after boiling.
Assemble and cook the dumplings promptly after making the dough. A small squeeze of lemon juice in the dough can slow discolouration slightly, though flavour is unaffected either way.

content.text: Potato dumplings in some form appear across the whole of Central Europe: German Kartoffelklöße, Austrian Knödel, Slovak halušky, Polish kopytka. The Czech bramborový knedlík is the expected companion to svíčková (beef sirloin in a cream and vegetable sauce, the Czech national dish) and to any rich, long-cooked preparation where the sauce needs something substantial alongside. Like the bread dumpling, the potato version is found at every Czech table where there is sauce to be absorbed.
